---
title: "A Nation Without Territory"
subtitle: "On collective capital and the self-organisation of the Belarusian diaspora"
description: "An essay on a new form of organising a distributed diaspora — an extraterritorial capitalised network capable of existing in an age when the role of human labour is in decline."
author: "Alexei Yerokhovets"
date: "2026-08-15"
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
categories: [essay, diaspora]
reading-time: true
lang: en
---

::: {.kicker}
Guest Essay · Specially for Aspen
:::

::: {.essay-title}
A Nation Without Territory
:::

::: {.essay-deck}
On collective capital and the self-organisation of the Belarusian diaspora.
:::

<hr class="essay-title-block-rule">

::: {.byline}
By [**Alexei Yerokhovets**]{} [August 15, 2026]{.sep} [18 min read]{.sep}
:::

::: {.dropcap-paragraph}
Precise data on the scale of Belarusian emigration after 2020 does
not exist. Different sources cite figures ranging from several
hundred thousand to more than a million people. In this case, however,
what matters is not statistical precision but the sheer scale of what
has happened. Over a few short years Belarus has lost a significant
share of its active, educated, and economically self-sufficient
population.
:::

There are also growing signs that this is not a temporary wave of
emigration, after which people will quickly return home, but the
formation of a new distributed diaspora — a significant portion of
which will remain outside Belarus for years or decades. Many have
already built new lives, brought their families, started businesses,
sent their children to schools in other countries. For others, return
remains emotionally desirable but an increasingly unrealistic
practical scenario.

Poland has become the principal destination of this emigration. It
is here that a particularly large number of Belarusians have
gathered — people who are close to one another in language, culture,
and historical experience. But along with opportunities, emigration
has brought a serious crisis of agency. Arriving in a new country, a
person often loses not only their familiar daily life but also the
social capital accumulated over years: professional reputation,
connections, access to financing, an intelligible system of trust.

As a result, a successful entrepreneur may suddenly find himself
unable to open a bank account or obtain a lease. A department head,
an engineer, or a specialist with many years of experience discovers
that on the new market his professional history means almost
nothing. People who were only recently creating jobs and managing
projects often find themselves forced to start life over with
low-skilled work and a constant sense of the provisionality of their
own position.

This emigration problem has coincided with a much larger process —
a global change in the technological order. The world is rapidly
entering an era in which human labour is gradually ceasing to be the
principal source of economic value. The development of artificial
intelligence, automation, and robotics is already beginning to
transform the labour market faster than states can adapt their social
and economic models.

Over the coming decades, more and more professions will either
disappear entirely or sharply lose their value. This means that the
classical life model of the modern person — education, work, salary,
pension — may prove as temporary a historical phenomenon as factory
labour in the nineteenth century. Increasingly, what will matter is
not the ability to sell one's working time but one's access to
capital and productive assets.

Major capital already operates by different rules. Wealthy people
rarely live on a salary. They own assets, draw on the growth of their
value, gain access to cheap credit secured by property, and preserve
capital within their structures, minimising its outflow. The ordinary
person, meanwhile, remains tied to a model in which a significant
share of the results of his labour is lost between taxes, inflation,
and the cost of access to basic services.

For Belarusian emigration this problem becomes doubled. We
simultaneously face the loss of national grounding and the start of
an era in which the very role of waged labour is becoming
increasingly fragile. Under these conditions, the attempt simply to
"integrate successfully" into existing systems may prove an
insufficient strategy even for individual survival — to say nothing
of preserving the community as such.

> If a nation no longer has territory, its principal asset must
> become collective capital and the capacity for self-organisation.

It is around this idea that this essay is built. It is not an
attempt to propose a utopia or to replace existing states. It is
rather a search for a new form of organising a distributed diaspora
— one that would allow Belarusians not only to survive individually
in different countries but gradually to become a network of people
capable of jointly defending their interests, creating capital,
supporting one another, and forming their own institutions in a
world where previous models of social organisation are rapidly losing
stability.

## Why the existing models do not work

Moving to another country is not in itself a catastrophe. Millions
of people around the world successfully emigrate, build new lives,
start businesses, get an education, and become part of new societies.
The problem of Belarusian emigration consists not so much in the
fact of relocation itself as in the absence of mechanisms for
collective adaptation and long-term self-organisation.

Poland has given Belarusians significantly more opportunities than
many other states. Here it has proved possible to legalise one's
status relatively quickly, open a business, find work, get an
education, or bring one's family. But it is important to acknowledge
the obvious: even the most well-disposed state primarily serves its
own interests and the interests of its own citizens.

The Polish state is not obliged to build a system of support for the
Belarusian diaspora that would compensate for the loss of social
connections, professional capital, and access to familiar
institutions. This is why many Belarusians continue to face problems
that cannot be solved purely through formal mechanisms of
integration: restrictions on banking services, difficulties obtaining
credit and leases, discrimination in the housing and labour markets,
the absence of political weight, and a weak understanding of one's
legal possibilities.

Individual successful integration does not, moreover, solve the
problem of the community as a whole. Many Belarusians have
undoubtedly been able to adapt: some have opened businesses, some
have built careers, some have obtained citizenship or attained a
high standard of living. But the success of individual people does
not yet amount to the emergence of a stable system. Individual
achievements do not create institutions, do not reduce the
vulnerability of others, and do not form collective agency.

Most existing émigré initiatives also have natural limits. Cultural
projects help preserve language and identity. Political organisations
perform an important representative function. Charitable initiatives
help people through crises. But almost none of the existing models
makes the creation of collective economic strength its principal
task.

A community that lacks its own capital, jobs, internal system of
trust, and infrastructure of mutual support inevitably remains
dependent on the external environment. As a result, emigration
exists more as an aggregate of individual people of similar origin
than as an organised system.

One of the principal problems of the Belarusian diaspora is
atomisation. We cooperate with one another economically relatively
rarely, build long-term horizontal connections with difficulty, and
possess almost no mechanisms of internal trust. Any joint project
quickly encounters either conflicts, mutual distrust, or an inability
to construct a stable system of governance. Within the emigration
even its own term has emerged — *bulbosrach*, an apt description of
the tendency to spend energy endlessly on internal conflicts instead
of accumulating collective strength.

The problem gradually extends, moreover, far beyond the emigration
itself. Belarusians who remain inside the country and Belarusians
abroad are diverging ever more sharply in life experience,
information environment, and everyday interests. The emigration
risks becoming a separate social stratum, gradually losing its
connection with the majority of Belarusians inside Belarus itself.
This is probably one of the most dangerous processes for the
long-term existence of the nation as a unified community.

The situation is further complicated by the fact that the world is
entering a period of serious economic transformation. The growth of
automation and artificial intelligence will intensify competition
for jobs and reduce the value of many kinds of human labour. Under
these conditions, the diaspora's fragmented existence makes it
particularly vulnerable.

History very rarely leaves room for neutral scenarios. Communities
unable to create their own institutions, mechanisms of cooperation,
and collective capital gradually dissolve into more organised
systems. This is a natural historical process and requires no one's
ill will.

This is why the Belarusian diaspora needs to begin the transition
from fragmented survival to institutionalised cooperation. Not as a
form of isolation from the surrounding society, but as a way to
transform from a set of separate émigrés into a community capable of
systematically defending, strengthening, and capitalising itself.

## The diaspora as a capitalised network

For most of human history, the nation was indissolubly tied to
territory. State borders defined the economy, the legal system,
education, communication, and a person's belonging to the community.
But the twenty-first century is gradually changing the very
principle of social organisation. Digital technologies, global
finance, remote work, and the development of artificial intelligence
are progressively reducing the importance of geography as the
determining factor of economic life.

The Belarusian diaspora already exists today as a distributed
network of people connected by a common language, culture, memory,
and professional ties. We live in different countries and work in
different jurisdictions, yet remain part of a single social space.
The problem is that this network as yet possesses almost none of its
own institutions, infrastructure of trust, or economic stability.

The modern person still lives in the logic of the industrial
twentieth century: education, work, salary, pension. But this model
is becoming less and less stable. The modern model of waged labour
secures a person's survival but relatively rarely allows him to
accumulate long-term independence. Moreover, a significant share of
the results of human labour is lost between taxes, the cost of
servicing state systems, and the constant rise in the cost of
living.

The modern economic elite, meanwhile, has long lived by different
rules. Major capital exists not on wages but on the ownership of
assets, the growth of their value, and access to cheap credit
secured by property. What is coming to matter more and more is not
the ability to sell one's time but one's participation in systems
that own productive assets.

For a distributed diaspora this opens up a fundamentally different
possibility of development. Instead of existing as a sum of separate
émigrés competing with one another for a place within other people's
systems, the Belarusian community could gradually begin building its
own economic infrastructure. Such a system could provisionally be
called *Veyshnoria Inc*[^1] — not a state in the classical sense, but
an extraterritorial corporate network uniting Belarusians regardless
of country of residence.

[^1]: Veyshnoria is a fictional country invented in 2017 in the
context of the Russian-Belarusian military exercises "Zapad-2017"
as a notional adversary of the Russia–Belarus Union State. On the
exercise maps it occupied the north-west of Belarus, roughly the
Grodno region and parts of the Minsk region. Unexpectedly for the
organisers, the idea of Veyshnoria became an internet meme: people
began producing flags, coats of arms, "passports", maps, a tongue-in-cheek
history, and even a "national identity" for it, often associating it
with a more European-oriented, liberal, and culturally distinctive
Belarus.

This is not about creating a utopia or attempting to replace
existing states. The police, criminal law, territorial security,
and most administrative functions will inevitably remain with
countries of residence. But a significant share of other functions
could exist within the distributed system itself: investments,
education, medical services, insurance, legal support, business
infrastructure, internal mechanisms of trust and arbitration.

The economic foundation of such a model could be the gradual
transformation of its participants from exclusively waged workers
into co-owners of collective capital. For example, a person would
continue to receive part of his income in ordinary money — necessary
for everyday life and for meeting the requirements of local
legislation. Another part could come in the form of a share in the
system — equity or an analogous instrument of participation in
collective capital.

In this model the principal priority becomes not maximum current
consumption but the growth in value of common assets. Unlike the
classical dividend model, which channels capital outwards and
creates additional tax burden, the system could aim at preserving
and continuously increasing capitalisation. Access to liquidity, in
turn, would be provided through credit secured against the assets
belonging to the participant — a mechanism long used by major
capital but almost inaccessible to the ordinary person.

The shares of such a system effectively begin to perform several
functions at once. They become both a form of savings and an
instrument of participation in the economic development of the
community, and collateral for access to financing, and part of a
long-term pension model. The person ceases to be exclusively a
seller of his own labour and begins to participate in the growth of
the system's productive assets.

This transformation becomes particularly important against the
backdrop of developments in artificial intelligence and robotics.
In the foreseeable future, the human being will probably cease to be
the basic productive unit of the economy. More and more functions,
from physical labour to analytical and managerial work, will be
performed by automated systems. This means that the very value of
human labour will inevitably begin to decline. Already today many
technological thinkers, including Sam Altman, are speaking of the
need to find new mechanisms for distributing benefits in a world
where a significant part of traditional employment may disappear.

Under these conditions, ownership of capital that invests in new
technologies and productive systems becomes not a question of luxury
but a question of long-term survival. A community that has managed
to turn its members into co-owners of such capital has a chance to
preserve its stability even in a scenario of deep crisis in the
traditional labour market.

It is crucially important that such a system not be limited to
emigration alone. One of the principal advantages of the
extraterritorial model is the possibility of involving Belarusians
regardless of their physical location. A person remaining in Belarus
can also participate in the economic life of the system, work on
its projects, invest, use its services, and become part of a common
space of trust and cooperation. Territory gradually ceases to be the
principal criterion of belonging to the community.

The practical sense of such a structure lies not in a symbolic
national idea, which every Belarusian inevitably understands in his
own way, but in the creation of real advantages for its
participants. The system must help a person become stronger: open
businesses, find work, gain access to education, medical services,
investments, legal protection, and professional connections. Within
it may emerge its own clinics, educational projects, leasing and
insurance companies, investment funds, and other services working
primarily in the interests of the community's participants.

The principal criterion of such a model's success should not be
political declarations or the number of events held, but the
community's capacity to create productive capital. The chief
criterion of the system's success should be its ability to create a
new economic reality: enterprises, jobs, investment mechanisms, and
its own infrastructure of development. Only economic agency allows a
community to preserve its stability over the long term.

Such a model carries serious risks, of course: concentration of
power, corruption, internal conflicts, populism, attempts to seize
control or to transform the system into a closed elite structure.
This is precisely why any mechanisms of such an organisation must
from the outset be built around transparency, voluntary
participation, separation of powers, and the possibility of constant
public oversight.

The very idea of a distributed capitalised network, however, no
longer looks like fantasy. The world is gradually moving toward an
era in which capital, technology, and the capacity for
self-organisation are beginning to play a greater role than territory
and traditional forms of state. Under these conditions, the
Belarusian diaspora can either remain a sum of scattered émigrés or
attempt to become a system capable of accumulating collective
strength and providing its participants with a significantly higher
level of stability, freedom, and mutual support.

## A transitional model: what can be built right now

The model described above may seem too ambitious or even utopian.
But the main mistake of most attempts at long-term design lies in
the desire to build the completed system all at once. In reality,
stable institutions almost always arise gradually — out of practical
solutions to specific problems.

This is why the path to a notional *Veyshnoria Inc* should begin not
with the creation of a "state of the future" but with the formation
of useful cooperative infrastructure within the existing diaspora.

First of all, one must acknowledge a simple thing: most people unite
not around ideology but around practical benefit. Over recent years,
the emigration has grown tired of endless political discussions,
conflicts, and struggles for symbolic leadership. A person who finds
himself in a new country usually needs much more down-to-earth
things — work, housing, legal help, professional connections, an
intelligible environment of trust, and the sense that he is not left
alone with his problems.

This is why usefulness must precede identity. Only practical value
can transform an abstract community into a stable cooperative
system.

Even relatively simple mutual-aid infrastructure can already
significantly strengthen the diaspora. This could involve creating a
system of employment recommendations, a database of vacancies and
verified employers, help with finding housing, consultations on
legalisation, psychological support, mentorship programmes for new
émigrés, and a network of verified specialists — lawyers,
accountants, doctors, contractors, and entrepreneurs.

The principal task of such infrastructure is to reduce the cost of
emigration for one's own. A person should not have to go through the
entire path of adaptation from scratch each time, losing years
searching for information, contacts, and trusted people.

But mutual aid alone is not enough. The principal deficit of the
emigration often turns out to be neither money nor even
opportunities, but trust. We are relatively bad at building
long-term cooperation, since we possess almost no mechanisms for
confirming reputation and reducing risks within the community. As a
result, even talented and professional people often prefer to act
alone, wary of conflicts, bad faith, or managerial chaos.

This is why one of the key tasks of the future system should be the
creation of internal reputational mechanisms. A history of
participation in projects, a system of recommendations, confirmed
professional reputation, mechanisms of mediation and voluntary
arbitration — all of this can substantially reduce transaction
costs within the community. The economy is always built on trust,
and trust, in turn, requires institutions.

The principal priority of such an organisation should be the
development of business and the accumulation of productive capital.
The community cannot judge its own success by the number of events,
declarations, or political statements. The only truly objective
criterion of stability remains the capacity to create enterprises,
jobs, investment mechanisms, and economic infrastructure.

The system must therefore consciously stimulate entrepreneurship
and cooperation. Help in finding partners, joint investments,
business incubation, support for startups, the bringing together of
professional competencies, and the creation of jobs for Belarusians
should be the central task of the organisation. Practical economic
benefit unites people significantly more effectively than abstract
ideological unity.

Over time, such a network may begin to produce its own services as
well. Some of these already look entirely realistic today: legal
and accounting support, recruitment and educational projects,
insurance and leasing mechanisms, medical services, investment
structures, or proprietary fintech solutions. It is important to
understand that this is not an attempt to artificially isolate
Belarusians inside a separate system, but the creation of
infrastructure that better accounts for the particularities and
needs of the community.

A new organisational architecture will also be required for the
sustainable operation of such a model. It must simultaneously remain
efficient enough for decision-making and transparent enough not to
turn into yet another closed vertical structure. One possible
solution might be a network of local *supolki* — small regional
communities with elected coordinators and limited authority. The
overall system could meanwhile employ modern digital instruments:
electronic voting, transparent reporting, open budgets, and
mechanisms of public oversight.

The problem of internal conflicts deserves separate attention. Any
long-term cooperation inevitably encounters disputes between
individuals, business partners, or project participants. In the
absence of trust, even small conflicts can destroy joint work and
strengthen the atomisation of the community. This is why the system
must contain a mechanism of voluntary mediation and arbitration. Its
task would not be to perform state functions, but to help in
resolving disputes and protecting the community's reputational
environment. The highest sanction in such a model could remain
exclusion from the network and loss of access to its possibilities.

All of this cannot, of course, be built at once. But it is
important to understand that the window of opportunity will not be
open indefinitely. With each passing year the emigration is
assimilating ever more, losing horizontal connections, and gradually
breaking up into separate groups and individual trajectories. At the
same time, global competition for resources, capital, and jobs is
intensifying in a world rapidly changing under the influence of
artificial intelligence and automation.

The self-organisation of the Belarusian diaspora is therefore
ceasing to be a question of cultural romanticism or political
activism. It is gradually becoming a question of the community's
long-term stability and of the Belarusians' capacity to preserve
agency in a world where the importance of the individual human being
as an economic unit will inevitably decline.

## Risks, limits, and the problem of human nature

Any attempt to build a long-term system sooner or later encounters
one and the same problem: the principal weakness of institutions
almost always turns out to be neither technology, nor economics, nor
even external pressure, but human nature itself. A conversation
about a new form of organising the diaspora would therefore be
incomplete without an honest conversation about the risks and limits
of such a model.

First of all, it is important to acknowledge the obvious: ideal
systems do not exist. Any human organisation eventually encounters
conflicts of interest, struggles for influence, bureaucratisation,
corruption, and the degradation of its original principles. The
history of states, corporations, religious organisations, and
political movements shows that practically any successful structure
gradually begins to gravitate toward self-preservation and the
concentration of power.

This is why the chief task of such a project lies not in creating a
utopia but in building mechanisms capable of restraining any
system's natural tendency toward closure and the abuse of power.

One of the principal risks will inevitably be the concentration of
control over collective resources. The more successful the system
becomes, the more strongly it will attract people interested not in
the development of the community but in gaining control over its
capital, infrastructure, and decision-making mechanisms. Any
distributed network over time risks becoming either a closed club or
a structure with "eternal leaders", gradually substituting the
interests of the community with their own.

The architecture of governance must therefore from the outset be
built around the limitation of power. Election, transparent budgets,
distributed governance, limited terms for functionaries, regular
rotation, and public oversight should be not decorative elements of
the system but its foundation.

Even this is not enough, however. The principal defence against
internal dictatorship in such a model remains the voluntariness of
participation. A person must retain the possibility of leaving the
system together with his assets and connections. Unlike a state,
which holds a person through territory, taxes, and an administrative
apparatus, such a network can exist only so long as its participants
consider their participation beneficial and fair.

Another serious threat is populism. Any system of collective capital
inevitably encounters pressure toward immediate distribution of
resources. Participants almost always want to receive more benefits
today, even if this destroys the long-term stability of the model.
History shows that consuming capital is significantly easier and
psychologically more attractive than accumulating it.

One of the most difficult tasks for *Veyshnoria Inc* should
therefore be the preservation of long-term investment logic even
under internal pressure. A system oriented exclusively toward quick
consumption will inevitably lose to communities capable of thinking
in generations.

A separate conversation is needed about the problem that the
Belarusian emigration has long described by the word *bulbosrach*.
This is not simply about conflicts as such, but about a chronic
inability to sustain cooperation for any length of time without
internal schism. Struggles for symbolic leadership, ideological
competition, moral categoricalness, and endless disputes about the
"correctness" of views can destroy joint projects faster than any
external pressure.

Perhaps this is one of the principal causes of the weakness of
post-Soviet societies in general. We are relatively bad at building
stable horizontal institutions, since we too often perceive
disagreement as a personal threat rather than as a normal part of
joint work.

For the long-term existence of such a system it is therefore
critically important to adopt the principle that practical value
matters more than ideological purity. The community must learn to
assess a person above all by his capacity to create useful projects,
jobs, capital, and infrastructure, rather than by the degree of his
conformity to ever-shifting political or cultural expectations.

There is also an opposite risk — the gradual transformation of the
system into a closed parallel society. Any strong internal network
eventually begins to create its own culture, mechanisms of trust,
and system of interaction. Without proper balance this can lead to
isolation, suspicion toward the outside world, and conflict with the
society of the country of residence.

This is why such a model must be built not on opposition to the
states and societies within which Belarusians live, but on
integration and mutual benefit. A strong and organised diaspora
should be perceived not as a threat but as a source of economic
activity, entrepreneurship, investment, and social stability.

Financial risks are no less serious. Any system based on collective
capital is vulnerable to crises, errors in management, declines in
asset value, technological changes, or sanctions pressure. Particular
caution will be needed with credit mechanisms and liquidity
management. An overly aggressive financial policy can destroy trust
in the system significantly faster than any ideological conflict.

For all the threats enumerated, however, one must acknowledge
another truth: refusing the attempt at self-organisation has its
own cost. If the Belarusian diaspora continues to exist exclusively
as a sum of separate individual trajectories, the most likely
scenario will be gradual assimilation, the breakdown of horizontal
connections, and the loss of collective agency. This is not a
tragedy and not anyone's fault — it is how the history of most
scattered communities unfolds.

The principal risk therefore lies not only in the possibility of
error in creating a new system, but in inaction. In a world where
scale, capital, and the capacity for cooperation are coming to play
an ever greater role, communities that do not create their own
institutions gradually lose the ability to influence even their own
future.

## The next step: from idea to discussion

It would be a mistake to take everything set out above as a ready
plan of action or a completed model of the diaspora's future
organisation. On the contrary, one of the main problems with such
projects lies precisely in the temptation to begin too early to
treat an abstract concept as an already finished system, while
overlooking its limits, internal contradictions, and potential risks.

A model of the kind sketched here as *Veyshnoria Inc* is in fact too
complex and multifaceted to be properly designed by a single person
or even a small group of like-minded people. It simultaneously
touches economics, law, technology, corporate governance, the
psychology of communities, questions of trust, identity, and the
long-term distribution of power. Any error in the architecture of
such a system can over time lead either to its disintegration or to
its gradual transformation into yet another ineffective and closed
structure.

This essay should therefore not be taken as a ready programme of
action. It is rather an attempt to articulate a direction of thought
and to flag a problem that is likely to become only more urgent in
the coming decades.

We are entering an era in which stability is ever more determined
not by territory but by the capacity for cooperation, the
accumulation of capital, and the creation of effective institutions.
If the Belarusian diaspora truly wants to preserve its agency over
the long term, it will inevitably have to learn to build such
institutions.

The principal difficulty here, however, lies not even in money or
technology. It is far harder to change the culture of interaction
itself. The post-Soviet space has left us a legacy of habits of
individual survival, distrust of horizontal structures, and the
constant expectation that any collective initiative will sooner or
later be either captured or destroyed by internal conflicts. The
creation of sustainable cooperation therefore requires not only
organisational decisions but also the gradual formation of a new
culture of joint action.

Perhaps the first and most realistic step today should be not the
creation of a large structure as such, but the appearance of an
environment for serious collective discussion of such ideas. A
space in which entrepreneurs, specialists, investors, lawyers, IT
developers, managers, and simply interested Belarusians could
jointly analyse risks, argue, test mechanisms, and gradually search
for viable forms of organisation.

Such work will almost inevitably require the emergence of a small
initiative group — something like "founding fathers" prepared to
take responsibility for the first organisational steps. But here it
is especially important to avoid the old mistake of many post-Soviet
projects — turning leaders into an untouchable political or moral
elite. Such a system needs not chieftains or heroes but people
capable of building institutions slowly and methodically.

It is no less important that such a project cannot be built around
ideological uniformity. Belarusians are too varied in their views,
experience, and conceptions of the future. Some associate themselves
above all with European integration, some with national culture,
some with entrepreneurship, and some are simply trying to secure a
stable life for their family. An attempt to construct unity around
the single "correct" system of views will almost inevitably lead to
a new schism.

This is perhaps why the principal unifying principle of such a
system should be not ideological identity but practical cooperation.
Not mandatory agreement on everything, but the capacity jointly to
create value, capital, jobs, infrastructure, and a space of mutual
trust.

The Belarusian diaspora today is gradually approaching the point at
which it will have to make a choice. Either to remain a sum of
separate individual stories of emigration, gradually dissolving
inside stronger and more organised systems. Or to attempt the
transition to a new form of existence — a distributed network of
people capable of jointly defending their interests, accumulating
collective capital, and building institutions that work in the
community's own interest.

The task itself may well be too large for a single generation. Any
long-term system begins at the moment when a sufficiently large
number of people first seriously conclude that acting together is
more sustainable than surviving alone.

---

::: {.essay-end}
*Alexei Yerokhovets*<br>
*August 2026*
:::
